unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 4! This unit is all about community and communication — the many ways people share news, ideas, and help with one another. You will read about a library service, a young reporter, and how a whole town shares information. You will learn strong new words like service, deliver, knowledge, information, discover, and priority, and you will use them to talk and write about your own community. By the end, you will be ready to share a message clearly, just like Amal does in this unit's story, “The Circular Plan”.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn four or five words each day and do the practice.
Work through the seven mini-lessons in the Grammar file, and check your answers.
Read the Story file, “The Circular Plan”, and answer the questions.
Come back here and work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see everything you have learned.

visual.alt: Children communicating while exploring services in their community

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Talk about services and communication in your community, using new Unit 4 words.
Describe places and things using There is and There are.
State facts about your town using the present simple (“Mail carriers deliver letters daily”).
Use countable and uncountable nouns correctly (much information, many books).
Give clear instructions using imperatives and connectives of sequence (First… Then… Finally…).
Write a set of instructions, a description, and a short report about your community.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Understand and enjoy the story “The Circular Plan” and talk about its message.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: The Library That Came to Us
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: In the town of Berbera, by the warm sea, there is a small library. It is a plain white building with a blue door, but inside it is full of treasure: shelves of fiction stories and shelves of true books packed with knowledge. The librarian, Mr. Isaac, wanted every child to read, even the children who lived far away. So he began a new service. Every Thursday, he loads books into a cart and travels to the villages to deliver them. “A library is not the building,” he says. “It is the books, and the people who read them. My priority is to bring information and good stories to everyone.” The children wait by the road. When they discover a new book they love, their faces shine like the sea at sunrise.

readings.title: Selma the Young Reporter
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Selma is nine years old, and she wants to be a scientist one day. But right now, her big challenge is her class newspaper. Every week, she must find real information about her town and share it. Last week, Selma learned that the population of her neighbourhood was growing, because many new families had arrived. “That is important news,” said her teacher. “Write it clearly, and do not judge the new families — welcome them.” Selma wrote her report neatly. She was careful to erase every mistake before she stuck the pages down. Her friend Ishmael tried to tease her: “A newspaper by a nine-year-old?” But when he read it, he stopped teasing. “This is high-quality work,” he said. “Can I help next week?”

readings.title: The Town Meeting
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “Good evening, everyone, and thank you for coming. Tonight I want to share some information about our new community centre. First, there is a reading room with many books. Then, there is a small clinic where a nurse gives health advice. Finally, there is an open hall for meetings and lessons. This is a service for all of us. Please spread the news, tell your neighbours, and bring your ideas. Communication is how a community grows strong.”

readings.title: The Circular Plan
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Amal lived in a small town near the coast of Ghana. Her school was plain on the outside, but it was full of energy inside. Every week, the teacher gave the class a new challenge — something to learn or build together as a team.
One morning, the teacher wrote on the board: “Project: Create a way to share information with the community.”
Amal raised her hand. “Like a newspaper?”
“Something like that,” the teacher said. “But you must use what you already know. Use your knowledge, and make something useful.”
The students talked during break. “We could build a big sign!” said Ishmael.
“No,” said Selma. “People will just walk past it. Let's make a box with papers inside.”
“That's still too plain,” Amal added. “It has to be circular. That's my rule!”
“Why circular?” Ishmael teased. “Is your brain a wheel?” Amal laughed, but she did not answer. She already had an idea.
After school, Amal went to the local library. She looked at the location of every fiction shelf, every news board, and every reading chair. She found a dusty corner near the door and smiled. “There is space here,” she whispered.
The librarian came over. “Can I help you?”
“We want to deliver news and stories to the whole town,” Amal replied.
“That's a big job,” the librarian said kindly. “But good service always starts small.”
The next day, Amal and her friends arrived early. They brought cardboard, tape, scissors, and markers.
“What are you building?” asked a younger boy.
“A circular news station!” Amal answered. “People will stop here and spin the circle. Each part gives different information — the weather, stories, or health tips.”
Ishmael frowned. “That sounds hard.”
“It's a challenge,” Amal agreed. “But we can do it.”
The teacher came by and nodded. “Very creative,” she said. “Now check your spelling before you stick anything down. And don't forget to erase the pencil lines.”
The group worked neatly. Amal drew the circle. Ishmael wrote the headlines. Selma added a funny comic about a scientist and a dancing goat, and everyone laughed.
“We need facts too,” Amal said, “not just fiction.”
“There is a doctor in town,” said Ishmael. “Let's write about health.”
“There are new shops opening as well,” added Selma.
“Write clearly. That is our top priority,” Amal reminded them.
By Friday, the project was ready. They placed it near the school gate. It stood tall and colourful.
The next morning, a group of parents and children gathered around it. “What is this?” asked a man.
“It's called ‘The Circular Plan',” Amal explained. “Turn it, and read what's on each side.”
A woman read aloud, “Tips for clean water.” A boy spun it and found the comic. “A goat that becomes a scientist?” he laughed.
“It's just fiction!” Ishmael shouted, grinning. People laughed and clapped. “This is good service,” someone said. “Our children are teaching us.”
One parent pointed. “But there is no news about the market. That's what people care about.”
“Then please help us write it!” Amal replied. “We want real thought from the people too.”
Another woman smiled. “That's a good priority — sharing everyone's voice.”
The teacher stood beside the project. “This,” she said, “is how we build a community. We learn, we teach, and we grow. Today, our students became leaders.”
As the crowd left, a boy began to tease Ishmael about his handwriting. “Your words look like little bugs!” he laughed. Ishmael grinned. “I wrote them in a hurry. I'll fix them later.”
“Good idea,” said Amal. “We must always check for quality.”
That afternoon, Amal looked at the circle again. “It may look plain,” she said to Selma, “but the work inside it matters.”
Samira nodded. “It's not about how big the quantity is,” she said. “It's about what people learn.”
And from that day on, people in Amal's town stopped by the spinning circle, learned something new, and sometimes even added their own page.

grammar.title: Countable and Uncountable Nouns
grammar.explanation: Some nouns we can count one by one — one book, two books, three books. We call these countable nouns. Other nouns we cannot count as separate items — like water, rice, or information. We call these uncountable nouns. Knowing the difference helps you choose the right words, so your sentences make sense.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Countable: use a number, or many / few.  “I have three apples.”  “There are many books.”
Uncountable: do not use a number. Use much, little, or some.  “I need some water.”  “We have much information.”
Use a or an only with singular countable nouns: “a book”, but never “a information”.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Can you give me some informations?”  ✗
Fix: information is uncountable, so it never adds an “s”: “Can you give me some information?”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: If you can put a number in front and add “s” (one apple, two apples), it is countable. If you would pour it or scoop it (water, rice, sugar), it is usually uncountable.
grammar.practice: Choose the correct word: | I have ______ (many / much) friends at school. | Could you give me ______ (some / many) information about the project? | There are ______ (few / little) cookies left in the jar. | I would like ______ (a / an) orange, please. | We need ______ (much / many) chairs for the party.

grammar.title: There is / There are
grammar.explanation: We use there is and there are to say that something exists. This helps us describe a place and share information about what is around us.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
There is → one thing (singular), or an uncountable noun.  “There is a library.”  “There is water in the glass.”
There are → two or more things (plural).  “There are three scientists in the lab.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “There is two cats on the wall.”  ✗
Fix: “cats” is plural, so use there are: “There are two cats on the wall.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think: is = 1 (one single star shining). are = more than one (many stars twinkling together).
grammar.practice: Choose the correct phrase: | ______ a bird in the tree. (is / are) | ______ three cars in the parking lot. (is / are) | ______ an elephant at the zoo. (is / are) | ______ many students in the classroom. (is / are)

grammar.title: Present Simple (Facts)
grammar.explanation: We use the present simple to talk about facts, habits, and things that are always true — like “Mail carriers deliver letters daily.” It helps you state true information clearly.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
For I, you, we, they: use the base verb.  “They deliver letters.”  “I read books.”
For he, she, it: add -s or -es.  “She plays.”  “He watches.”
You can add time words: daily, often, every day, sometimes.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She play soccer every day.”  ✗
Fix: the subject is “she”, so add “s”: “She plays soccer every day.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: He, She, It → don't forget the “s”! For everyone else, just use the plain base verb.
grammar.practice: Write the correct present simple form of the verb in brackets: | Dogs ______ (bark) when they see strangers. | She ______ (study) every day after school. | Fish ______ (swim) in water. | My brother ______ (play) football on weekends. | The Earth ______ (revolve) around the Sun.

grammar.title: Passive Voice
grammar.explanation: In the active voice, the subject does the action: “The cat chased the mouse.” In the passive voice, the subject receives the action: “The mouse was chased by the cat.” We use the passive when we want to focus on the action, or on who it happened to, rather than who did it.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Move the object to the front: “The teacher reads the book” → “The book …”
Add the correct form of to be (is, are, was, were): “The book is …”
Add the past participle of the verb: “The book is read …”
Add the doer with by only if you want to: “The book is read by the teacher.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “The meal is cook by the chef.”  ✗
Fix: use the past participle: “The meal is cooked by the chef.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Ask: “What happens to the subject?” If the subject receives the action, you are using the passive voice.
grammar.practice: Change each active sentence into the passive voice: | The artist paints the picture. | The team won the game. | The students finished their homework.

grammar.title: Connectives of Sequence
grammar.explanation: Connectives of sequence are words that show the order of steps or events — First, Next, Then, After that, Finally. They make instructions and stories easy to follow.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Begin the step with a connective: First, …  Then, …  Finally, …
Put a comma after the connective when it starts a sentence.
Keep the steps in the correct order so the reader can follow.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “First discover the problem.”  ✗  (no comma)
Fix: put a comma after the connective: “First, discover the problem.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of the connectives as a treasure map — each one leads you to the next step. Don't skip a step!
grammar.practice: Fill each blank with a sequence connective (First, Next, Finally): | ______ , wash your hands. | ______ , dry your hands with a towel. | ______ , enjoy your snack!

grammar.title: Imperatives + Descriptive Starters
grammar.explanation: Imperatives are commands or instructions that tell someone to do something — “Sit down”, “Erase your mistakes”. They help us communicate clearly and directly.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use the base verb with no “to”:  Clean.  Erase.  Listen.
Add details if you like:  “Erase your mistakes neatly!”
To say NOT to do something, start with Don't:  “Don't run in the hall.”
Add please to be polite:  “Please close the door.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “To clean your room.”  ✗
Fix: imperatives use the base verb with no “to”: “Clean your room.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: An imperative is a friendly “do” command. It usually starts with the action word itself.
grammar.practice: Complete each command with the base form of the verb in brackets: | ______ (eat) your vegetables! | ______ (close) the window, please. | ______ (not talk) during the film. | ______ (help) your friend with the project. | ______ (bring) your book to class tomorrow. | Lesson 7:  Descriptive Starters | What it means and why it matters | A descriptive starter puts a describing word right at the front of your sentence, near the noun. Instead of “The service is good,” you can say “This high-quality service is very helpful.” It makes your writing more interesting and clear. | How to form and use it | Choose a noun: “service”. | Choose a describing word: “high-quality”. | Put them together at the start: “This high-quality service …” | Finish the sentence: “This high-quality service is very helpful.” | A common mistake | Mistake: “This high-quality services are …”  ✗  (singular and plural mixed up) | Fix: match a singular noun with a singular verb: “This high-quality service is …”  ✓ | Memory tip | Think of a descriptive starter as “dressing up” your sentence — the describing word is the nice outfit your noun wears at the front. | Practice 7 | Add a describing word at the start of each sentence: | ______ dog is playful. | ______ book is interesting. | ______ game is exciting.

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Describe a Service in Your Community
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Think of a service in your community: a shop, a mosque, a clinic, a market, or a library. Get ready to say three or four sentences about it. Use these sentence starters:
“In my community, there is a ______.”
“The location of it is ______.”
“People go there to ______.”
“I think this service is ______ (helpful / important / friendly).”
Record yourself. Check: Did you use there is correctly? Did you speak in clear, full sentences?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise describe a service in your community and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Give Clear Instructions
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Teach someone how to do a simple task — how to make tea, how to wash your hands, or how to send a message. Use imperatives and sequence words:
“First, ______.”
“Then, ______.”
“After that, ______.”
“Finally, ______.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise give clear instructions and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Twenty Questions: Guess the Job
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family. One person thinks of a community helper (a scientist, a teacher, a nurse, a mail carrier, a shopkeeper). The others ask yes/no questions to guess it:
Ask: “Do you deliver things?”  “Do you work with knowledge and books?”
The thinker answers only “Yes, I do” or “No, I don't.”
Guess the job in twenty questions or fewer. Then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise twenty questions: guess the job and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5: Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6: Three-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a three-minute talk using an opening, three connected ideas with evidence or examples, and a purposeful closing.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise three-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1: Instructions: “How to Share News in My Community”
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a set of instructions with at least four steps. Use a sequence word to begin each step, and use imperatives (action words). For example:
First, find real information. Then, write it clearly. After that, check your spelling. Finally, share it with your neighbours.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about instructions: “how to share news in my community”.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 2: Description: “A Circular Object in Nature”
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write four or five sentences describing something circular you have seen — the sun, the moon, a drum, a plate, a wheel. Begin with a descriptive starter: “This bright, circular moon is…”
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about description: “a circular object in nature”.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 3: A Short Report: “My Town”
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write three or four sentences of true information about your town. You could write about its population, its location, or a service people use. Use the present simple for facts.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about a short report: “my town”.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 4: Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about the unit. Begin with a topic sentence and join ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about connected paragraph.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 5: Information Text
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five accurate facts. Use precise unit vocabulary and check spelling and punctuation.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about information text.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 6: Reflect and Improve
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose earlier writing, revise three sentences, add two useful details, and explain one improvement you made.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about reflect and improve.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

activities.title: Activities 1: Match the Word to its Meaning
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each word (1–5) to its meaning (a–e).
service
deliver
knowledge
population
priority
a) the number of people living in a place
b) something that is more important than other things
c) help or work done for others
d) to bring something to a person or place
e) what you know or learn
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2: There is or There are?
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Choose the correct phrase.
______ a library near my house.
______ many books on the shelf.
______ some information on the board.
______ three scientists in the lab.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3: much or many?
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Choose much (for uncountable nouns) or many (for countable nouns).
How ______ books are in the library?
How ______ information did you find?
There are too ______ people in the hall.
We do not have ______ time left.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4: Put the Steps in Order
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Add the sequence words First, Then, After that, Finally to these steps for sharing a message:
______ , decide what you want to say.
______ , write it down clearly.
______ , check your spelling.
______ , give the message to the right person.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5: Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6: Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six important details in the unit texts. Record the detail and explain what it helps the reader understand.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: Community and Communication learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit Writing 1: Instructions: “How to Share News in My Community” and a recording of Speaking 1: Describe a Service in Your Community. Improve both pieces using the Grade 4 rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does 'service' mean?
quizzes.options: An idea or opinion in your mind. | Facts or details about something. | Help or work done for others. | To find or learn something new.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Help or work done for others.
quizzes.explanation: The library started a free book service for the town.

quizzes.question: What does 'deliver' mean?
quizzes.options: An idea or opinion in your mind. | To bring something to a person or place. | To find or learn something new. | What you know or learn.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To bring something to a person or place.
quizzes.explanation: The mail carrier will deliver our letters.

quizzes.question: What does 'information' mean?
quizzes.options: Facts or details about something. | Something difficult; or, to test someone. | To find or learn something new. | What you know or learn.
quizzes.explanation: The website has useful information about our town.

quizzes.question: What does 'thought' mean?
quizzes.options: An idea or opinion in your mind. | Something difficult; or, to test someone. | Something more important than other things. | What you know or learn.
quizzes.correctAnswer: An idea or opinion in your mind.
quizzes.explanation: She shared her thought about the new plan.

quizzes.question: What does 'discover' mean?
quizzes.options: How good or bad something is. | Something difficult; or, to test someone. | Something more important than other things. | To find or learn something new.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To find or learn something new.
quizzes.explanation: Scientists discover new plants in the forest.

quizzes.question: What does 'knowledge' mean?
quizzes.options: How good or bad something is. | Something more important than other things. | To decide if something is good or bad; a person who decides in court. | What you know or learn.

quizzes.question: What does 'challenge' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who studies science. | How good or bad something is. | Something difficult; or, to test someone. | To decide if something is good or bad; a person who decides in court.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Something difficult; or, to test someone.
quizzes.explanation: The maths test was a big challenge.

quizzes.question: What does 'priority' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who studies science. | Something more important than other things. | Stories that are made up and not real. | To decide if something is good or bad; a person who decides in court.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Something more important than other things.

quizzes.question: What does 'quality' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who studies science. | A place where something is. | How good or bad something is. | Stories that are made up and not real.
quizzes.correctAnswer: How good or bad something is.
quizzes.explanation: This work is made with high quality.

quizzes.question: What does 'judge' mean?
quizzes.options: A place where something is. | Shaped like a circle. | Stories that are made up and not real. | To decide if something is good or bad; a person who decides in court.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To decide if something is good or bad; a person who decides in court.
quizzes.explanation: Do not judge a book by its cover.

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you read the two passages with me and explain any hard words?”
“Can you ask me questions about the library story to check I understood?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you read the town-meeting talk aloud so I can listen?”
“Can you play a listening game where I have to catch the sequence words?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me practise describing a service in my town?”
“Can you play ‘Guess the Job’ with me?”
“Can you check that I used ‘there is’ and ‘there are’ correctly?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my instructions and tell me if my steps are in a clear order?”
“Can you help me make my description more interesting?”
“Can you check that I used countable and uncountable nouns correctly?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on these six words?”
“Can you give me another sentence using the word ‘knowledge’?”
“What is the difference between ‘information’ and ‘knowledge’?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me use ‘challenge’ and ‘priority’ in sentences?”
“What does it mean to ‘judge a book by its cover’?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on these describing words?”
“Can you help me describe the location of my school?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“What is the difference between kind teasing and unkind teasing?”
“Can you use the word ‘tease’ in a friendly sentence?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my grammar answers?”
“Why do we say ‘much information’ but ‘many books’?”
“Can you give me five more sentences to practise the passive voice?”

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Help or work done for others. The library started a free book service for the town.
To bring something to a person or place. The mail carrier will deliver our letters.
Facts or details about something. The website has useful information about our town.
An idea or opinion in your mind. She shared her thought about the new plan.
To find or learn something new. Scientists discover new plants in the forest.
What you know or learn. Reading books gives us knowledge.
Something difficult; or, to test someone. The maths test was a big challenge.
Something more important than other things. Safety is our top priority.
How good or bad something is. This work is made with high quality.
To decide if something is good or bad; a person who decides in court. Do not judge a book by its cover.
